topic.title: HTML Sectioning Content
topic.description: Sectioning elements divide pages into navigable regions that screen readers and search engines use.

content.text: Sectioning Content: section, article, and the Document Outline

text.value: that create discrete regions of content with implied semantic roles. The four main sectioning elements are
in the document outline, meaning heading levels restart within them relative to their nesting depth. A
groups thematically related content that belongs to the surrounding document. An
contains content that is independently distributable or reusable (a blog post, a product card, a news story).
holds content tangentially related to the surrounding content. All four are
that screen reader users can jump between directly. Reference:

rows: Introductory content for a section or article
Closing content for a section or article

content.title: section Gets No ARIA Role Unless It Has an Accessible Name
content.text: An unlabelled <section> element has no ARIA landmark role and is invisible to screen reader navigation. A <section> only becomes a 'region' landmark when it has an accessible name via aria-labelledby pointing to its heading id, or via aria-label. Without a name, screen readers treat the section as an anonymous grouping, giving it no more landmark value than a <div>. Always label sections you want users to be able to navigate to directly.

examples.title: Complete Page Layout Using All Four Sectioning Elements
examples.description: This example builds a realistic page structure using section, article, nav, and aside in correct nesting. It demonstrates labelled sections becoming region landmarks, article elements for individual cards, nav with aria-label for multiple navigation regions, and aside for supplementary sidebar content.

examples.output: Page renders a full learning platform layout. The primary nav in the header has an aria-label distinguishing it from the sidebar nav. The main column shows two labelled sections (Featured Courses and Recent Articles), each containing article elements styled in green. Hovering any article reveals the green border. The sidebar aside shows learning paths and popular tags with a second nav labelled distinctly. Screen reader landmark lists would show: banner, navigation (Primary), main, region (Featured Courses), region (Recent Articles), complementary (Learning paths), navigation (Learning path), contentinfo.

examples.title: Nested Articles, section vs div Decision, and Document Outline
examples.description: This example shows article elements nested inside article (comments inside a blog post), the document outline produced by correct sectioning, and a side-by-side comparison of the same layout built with section versus div to show the landmark difference.

Sectioning Patterns Reference

1. Nested article elements: post with comments

What is the CSS Cascade?

Priya Sharma · 1 March 2025

The cascade determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element. It weighs origin, specificity, and source order to resolve conflicts.

Thanks Arjun! ID specificity is one of the most common sources of confusion in CSS.

2. Document outline produced by the above

h1: Sectioning Patterns Reference └ article (article) └ h2: What is the CSS Cascade? └ section (region: "Comments") └ h3: Comments (2) └ article (article): Arjun Nair comment └ h4: Arjun Nair └ article (article): author reply └ h4: Priya Sharma (author) └ article (article): Meera Pillai comment └ h4: Meera Pillai

3. section vs div: landmark visibility

div grouping — no landmark, invisible to screen readers

<div>  <!-- no role, no landmark -->
  <h2>Upcoming Webinars</h2>
  <p>...</p>
</div>

section with aria-labelledby — named region landmark

<section aria-labelledby="webinars-h">
  <h2 id="webinars-h">Upcoming Webinars</h2>
  <p>...</p>
</section>
<!-- Screen reader landmarks: region "Upcoming Webinars" -->

Unlabelled section — still no landmark role

<section>  <!-- no accessible name = no region landmark -->
  <h2>Upcoming Webinars</h2>
  <p>...</p>
</section>

4. When to use div instead of section

Use div for styling containers with no semantic meaning

<!-- Grid layout wrapper: no semantic purpose -->
<div class="card-grid">
  <article>...</article>
  <article>...</article>
</div>

<!-- Decoration or JS hook: no semantic purpose -->
<div class="modal-overlay" role="dialog">...</div>
examples.output: Page renders four sections. The nested article section shows a blog post with a bordered left accent, containing a comments region with two comment articles (one with a nested reply article). The document outline section shows the accessibility tree produced by the nesting. The section vs div section shows three code cards: a div (no landmark), a labelled section (region landmark), and an unlabelled section (still no landmark). The fourth section shows correct use of div for layout wrappers and modal containers with no semantic meaning.

subtopics.title: Section Element
subtopics.description: The section element groups thematically related content and becomes a region landmark when labelled.

text.value: element represents a thematic grouping of content that belongs to the document but forms a distinct unit within it. It is appropriate when the content could have its own entry in a
ARIA landmark role, making it discoverable by screen reader users navigating landmarks. Without an accessible name it has no landmark role, identical in the accessibility tree to a
should contain at least one heading element to label its content. Reference:

content.title: section vs div: The Semantic Decision
content.text: Use section when the content is thematic and could logically appear in a table of contents: 'Introduction', 'Features', 'Pricing', 'FAQ'. Use div when the grouping is purely structural: a flex container, a modal wrapper, a JavaScript hook. The test is whether the grouping has semantic meaning beyond layout. If it does, section. If not, div.

examples.title: Labelled Sections as Region Landmarks
examples.description: This example shows a documentation page with three sections, each labelled with aria-labelledby pointing to their heading id. It demonstrates the resulting landmark structure, the section without a label that produces no landmark, and the correct use of section versus div for layout containers.
examples.code_snippet:
HTML Flexbox Guide

Introduction

Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout model that distributes space and aligns items along a single axis. It solves common layout problems that were difficult with floats.

Core Concepts

The flex container is the parent element with display: flex. Direct children become flex items. The main axis runs in the direction of flex-direction.

Key Properties

  • justify-content: align along the main axis
  • align-items: align along the cross axis
  • flex-wrap: allow items to wrap to new lines

Practical Examples

A navigation bar, a card grid, and a split-pane layout are the three most common Flexbox patterns in real projects.

Further Reading

See the MDN Flexbox documentation and the CSS Tricks Complete Guide to Flexbox for deeper coverage.

Landmark structure produced

banner (header) main └ region "Introduction" └ region "Core Concepts" └ region "Key Properties" └ region "Practical Examples" [unlabelled section: no landmark, not listed]

section vs div for a layout wrapper

Wrong: section as a layout container

<section class="card-grid"> — no semantic grouping

Right: div for layout, section for semantic grouping

<div class="card-grid"> — layout wrapper
<section aria-labelledby="courses-h"> — semantic region
examples.output: Page renders an HTML Flexbox Guide with four sections. Three sections have visible blue left borders and aria-labelledby attributes. The nested section inside Core Concepts shows a sub-region. The unlabelled Further Reading section has the same visual style but appears in the landmark map as absent. The landmark map shows the three named region landmarks and the nested sub-region. The section vs div comparison shows the wrong and correct uses with code snippets.

text.value: element exists on a spectrum between

(fully independent content). It is the right choice when content belongs to the surrounding document context but forms a distinct enough unit to warrant its own heading and landmark. Think of chapters in a book: each chapter belongs to the book but can be named and navigated to directly.
does not add new content. It creates an association between the section and a heading that already exists inside it. Screen readers use this association to name the landmark region. The heading
value must match exactly. A typo in either will silently break the landmark name, leaving the section unnamed despite the attribute being present.
landmarks on a page to those that are genuinely useful navigation destinations. WCAG recommends that

landmarks are used sparingly. Too many named sections clutter the screen reader landmark list and make navigation harder, not easier. A typical documentation page needs three to six named regions at most.

subtopics.title: Article Element
subtopics.description: The article element wraps self-contained content that can be independently distributed or reused.

text.value: piece of content that could stand alone: a blog post, a news story, a product card, a forum comment, a tweet, or a widget. The defining test is

: if the content could be extracted from its current page and syndicated in an RSS feed, published on another site, or embedded in a different context while still making complete sense, it belongs in an
. Articles can be nested: a comments section inside a blog post article contains individual comment articles. Every

Independently meaningful with author and date
Only meaningful in context of the product page
Yes if it has title, description, and metadata; No if it is just an img

examples.title: Blog Post Article, Product Cards, and Comment Thread
examples.description: This example shows three article patterns: a long-form blog post article with header and footer elements inside it, a product card grid where each card is an article, and a comment thread using nested articles. It demonstrates how article header and footer are scoped to the article, not the page.
examples.code_snippet:
Article Element Patterns

1. Blog post article with header and footer

CSS

Mastering CSS Custom Properties

By Priya Sharma · Published 1 March 2025 · 8 min read

CSS custom properties (variables) allow values to be defined once and reused throughout a stylesheet. They cascade like any other CSS property and can be updated dynamically with JavaScript.

Unlike preprocessor variables (Sass, Less), custom properties are resolved at runtime in the browser, enabling themes and dark mode without recompilation.

Tags: CSS, Custom Properties, Theming

2. Product card grid (each card is an article)

examples.output: Page renders three article patterns. The blog post shows a bordered card with a scoped header containing the tag, title, and byline, main content, and a scoped footer with tags. The product grid shows three course cards each as an article with a gradient image area, title, price, and link. The comment thread shows two comment articles inside a labelled section, the first with a nested reply article using a lighter blue left border. All heading levels are correct relative to their nesting depth.
